chief_complaint: A 45-year-old male patient presented with sudden onset of severe abdominal pain and melena.
history: The patient underwent endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration (EUS-FNA) 2 weeks prior to admission. He had no significant past medical history and was not taking any medications. He denied any history of trauma or recent travel.
exam: Vital signs: blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, pulse 120 beats per minute, respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 92% on room air. Physical examination revealed a distended abdomen with tenderness in the left upper quadrant.
labs: Laboratory results: hemoglobin 6.2 g/dL, hematocrit 19.2%, platelet count 150,000/μL, and coagulation studies were within normal limits. Imaging studies: CT angiography showed a 2.5 cm splenic artery aneurysm.
correct_diagnosis: Splenic artery aneurysm following endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration
differentials: Splenic artery aneurysm following endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration
Gastrointestinal bleeding due to peptic ulcer disease
correct_ranking: Splenic artery aneurysm following endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration
Gastrointestinal bleeding due to peptic ulcer disease
